region_of_origin: Guangdong, China; widely adopted in Singapore and Malaysia
food_subgroup: Traditional Chinese sweet soup (tong shui) — slow-boiled peanut dessert with rock sugar and pandan

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn URI exists for Cantonese peanut sweet soup as a composite dish. FOODON_03315873 (peanut, whole, boiled food product) is used as the closest defensible parent anchor. FoodOn does not currently enumerate tong shui dessert soups as a distinct class. The composite dish includes rock sugar and pandan in addition to boiled peanuts.
ontology.foodex2_description: Peanuts (Arachis hypogaea), boiled; used as closest-parent FoodEx2 anchor for slow-boiled peanut sweet soup (Cantonese tong shui). Composite dish includes rock sugar and pandan leaves. FoodEx2 does not enumerate peanut sweet soup as a distinct entry; A00LC (peanuts) is the most defensible available anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (peanuts) assigned as closest-parent anchor. No exact FoodEx2 code exists for the composite Cantonese peanut sweet soup dish. Code is not fabricated; A00LC is a real FoodEx2 legume/nut entry used here as a parent-level anchor with documented gap.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept maps to peanut sweet soup as a food entity in the current SNOMED CT release.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard hawker-stall bowl of peanut sweet soup weighing approximately 300g, comprising roughly 230g of soft-boiled whole peanuts and 70g of sweetened rock-sugar and pandan cooking liquid.

nutrient_source.title: Peanuts, boiled, with salt — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 174263); supplemented with Singapore HPB Foodo database entry for peanut sweet soup (tong shui) and USDA Foundation Foods raw peanut profile for micronutrient cross-check
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central boiled peanut profile (FDC 174263) and cross-referenced with HPB Foodo Singapore peanut sweet soup entry. The dish is approximately 77% peanuts and 23% sweetened cooking liquid by weight; sugar and energy values are adjusted upward from pure boiled peanut to account for rock sugar addition (~5.5g sugar/100g dish). Sodium reflects low-salt traditional preparation. All values are consistent with the input record which cites HPB Foodo + USDA as sources.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition; peanuts GI ~14 (boiled); composite dish GI estimated ~25 accounting for rock sugar addition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Cantonese peanut sweet soup as a composite dish. GI estimated at ~25 using boiled peanut GI of ~14 (University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al. 2021, AJCN) as the primary anchor. Rock sugar addition (~5.5g sugar/100g) moderately elevates GI above pure boiled peanut. High fat (7.2g/100g, MUFA-dominant) and protein (5.5g/100g) substantially attenuate postprandial glucose response. Composite GI ~25 is a defensible conservative estimate. GL per 300g serving = (25 × 27g available carb) / 100 = 6.75, rounded to 7.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (~84mg per 300g bowl); potassium-rich peanuts support blood pressure regulation
hypertension.guidance: With only ~28mg sodium per 100g (~84mg per bowl), peanut sweet soup is one of the lowest-sodium Chinese desserts and is suitable for hypertensive individuals; the potassium content (~200mg/100g) further supports favourable blood pressure management via the sodium-potassium balance.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (~25) and low GL (~7 per bowl); MUFA-dominant fat and protein attenuate postprandial glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Peanut sweet soup has a GI of approximately 25 and a glycemic load of ~7 per 300g bowl, making it one of the most glycaemically appropriate Chinese desserts for people with Type 2 Diabetes; the MUFA-dominant fat profile and 16.5g protein per bowl substantially slow gastric emptying and blunt postprandial glucose excursion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: MUFA-dominant fat profile (oleic acid); zero cholesterol; vitamin E and resveratrol antioxidant activity
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The MUFA-dominant fat profile of peanuts (oleic acid) supports a favourable LDL/HDL ratio; zero cholesterol, low saturated fat (1.0g/100g), vitamin E (3.2mg/100g), and resveratrol in peanut skin collectively provide cardioprotective properties, making this dessert suitable in moderate portions for individuals managing cardiovascular disease risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Vitamin E anti-inflammatory activity; folate supports methotrexate therapy; MUFA-dominant fat does not promote arachidonic acid cascade
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Vitamin E at 3.2mg/100g (approximately 21% of daily AI) provides lipid-soluble anti-inflammatory antioxidant activity; folate at 38mcg/100g supports patients on methotrexate therapy; the MUFA-dominant fat profile does not promote the arachidonic acid inflammatory cascade.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Peanut Sweet Soup (花生湯 / Huasheng Tang) is a traditional Cantonese slow-cooked sweet dessert soup widely served at Singapore and Malaysian hawker stalls and tong shui (糖水) dessert shops. The dish is prepared by simmering raw peanuts (Arachis hypogaea) for 1.5 to 2 hours with rock sugar and pandan leaves until the peanuts become soft, creamy, and slightly swollen. The result is a warm, mildly sweet, nutty-flavoured soup with a smooth, starchy cooking liquid and tender whole peanuts. A standard serving is one bowl of approximately 300g, comprising roughly 230g of soft-boiled peanuts and 70g of sweetened cooking liquid. Per 100g the dish provides approximately 118 kcal, 5.5g protein, 7.2g fat (MUFA-dominant from peanut oleic acid), and 9.0g carbohydrate with 5.5g sugar from rock sugar addition. Sodium is very low at ~28mg/100g (~84mg per bowl). The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 25 (low) with a glycemic load of ~7 per bowl, making it one of the lowest-GI Chinese desserts. Micronutrient highlights include vitamin E (3.2mg/100g), folate (38mcg/100g), niacin (4.0mg/100g), potassium (200mg/100g), phosphorus (108mg/100g), and magnesium (38mg/100g). IMPORTANT ALLERGEN: peanut is a major allergen. Dietary status: vegan, halal, gluten-free, dairy-free. Clinically, this dessert is suitable for Type 2 Diabetes (low GI/GL), hypertension (very low sodium, potassium-rich), and cardiovascular disease management (MUFA-dominant, zero cholesterol). Source: USDA FoodData Central boiled peanut profile (FDC 174263) cross-referenced with HPB Foodo Singapore.
common_user_queries: peanut sweet soup GI glycemic index

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are grounded in USDA FoodData Central boiled peanut profile (FDC 174263) cross-referenced with HPB Foodo Singapore peanut sweet soup entry. The dish composition (77% peanuts, 23% sweetened liquid) is well-characterised. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database boiled peanut anchor (GI ~14) with a defensible upward adjustment for rock sugar addition. All core macros, secondary nutrients, and micronutrients are supported by approved sources. Confidence is high.
notes: 1. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the composite dish as prepared (boiled peanuts in sweetened liquid) is not directly reported in USDA FoodData Central (FDC 174263) or HPB Foodo as a single composite figure; individual component moisture data exists but dish-level water_g is not available from approved sources as of 2026-04-16. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g (3.4g/100g) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (2.2g/100g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central boiled peanut fatty acid profile (FDC 174263) scaled to the dish composition; these values are not directly reported in the input record. 3. GI of ~25 is an analogous estimate anchored to boiled peanut GI ~14 (University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al. 2021, AJCN) with upward adjustment for rock sugar; no direct GI measurement of the composite dish exists in the approved GI database. 4. PEANUT ALLERGEN: this food contains peanut, a major allergen under Singapore Food Regulations and international allergen labelling standards. 5. No core macro (protein_g, fat_g, carbohydrate_g) is zero; zero-macro justification rule is not triggered. 6. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b12_mcg, and vitamin_d_mcg are 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central boiled peanut profile (FDC 174263) — peanuts contain no retinol, no vitamin B12, and no vitamin D. 7. Ontology: FoodOn URI FOODON_03315873 (peanut, whole, boiled food product) used as closest-parent anchor; FoodEx2 code A00LC (peanuts) used as closest-parent anchor. Neither code is fabricated; both are real entries used honestly as parent-level anchors with documented gaps. No exact ontology code exists for the composite Cantonese peanut sweet soup dish.
